content.plain_english: This week your child is meeting UK coins and notes for the first time. They are learning the names — penny, two pence, five pence — and counting amounts in coins.
content.in_school: Children handle real or plastic coins, sorting them and saying the value out loud. They count amounts up to 20 p using 1 p, 2 p and 5 p coins. They begin to swap small coins for a bigger one — five 1 p coins for a 5 p.
content.home_activity: Empty a small handful of coins onto the table. Ask your child to find a 1 p, a 2 p, a 5 p and a 10 p. Then ask them to make 10 p in two different ways. Two minutes — spread them out, no pressure.
dinner_table_questions: Which coin is bigger in value — a 2 p or a 5 p?
How many 1 p coins make a 5 p?
If I have a 10 p and a 2 p, how much is that?

key_vocabulary.definition: A round piece of metal money.
A paper or plastic piece of money — like £5 or £10.
The smaller unit of UK money.
The larger unit. £1 is the same as 100 p.
How much a coin or note is worth.

example_questions: Name three different coins in the UK.
How many 2 p coins make 10 p?
If you have a 5 p and a 1 p, how much do you have?
content.times_table_tip: Year 1 children practise counting in 2s, 5s and 10s. Money is the perfect place to count in 5s — 5 p, 10 p, 15 p, 20 p. Lay out four 5 p coins and count up.
